17: A fulfillment: he took our infirmities and bore our diseases. Matthew's citation of Isaiah 53:4 at the healing ministry — not at the cross — reveals that the Servant's vicarious bearing is already operative in the healing miracles. Christ carries what he heals: he takes it from the sufferer and bears it himself. The cross is not the only moment of substitution but its culmination; every healing is an anticipation of the cross in which the Servant absorbs what afflicts humanity. The miracles of Matthew 8-9 are the Servant Song enacted in the villages of Galilee.
1: A theme: "When he came down from the mountain, great crowds followed him." The descent from the mountain echoes Moses descending from Sinai (Exodus 34:29-32); Moses radiated with the glory of having met God, while Jesus descended as God himself. The great crowds following him fulfill the prophetic image of the nations streaming to the mountain of the LORD (Isaiah 2:2-4; Micah 4:1-2) — the eschatological gathering of the nations to the mountain of divine instruction, now happening around the New Moses.
2: A fulfillment: "And behold, a leper came to him and knelt before him, saying, 'Lord, if you will, you can make me clean.'" Leprosy (or skin disease — tzara'at) rendered the afflicted person ritually unclean and excluded from the covenant community (Leviticus 13:45-46). The healing of a leper by YHWH was considered a divine prerogative — Miriam's healing (Numbers 12:10-15) and Naaman's (2 Kings 5:1-14) were both presented as exceptional divine acts. Jesus healing lepers fulfills the messianic restoration in Isaiah 35:3-6 and his own answer to John's disciples (Matthew 11:5: "lepers are cleansed").
3: A direct fulfillment: "And Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, saying, 'I will; be clean.' And immediately his leprosy was cleansed." The touch of a leper would render a person ritually impure; Jesus' touch renders the leper clean. The pattern reverses the normal flow of contamination: the holy cleanses the unclean rather than the unclean contaminating the holy. This is the logic of the Incarnation itself — Christ takes on flesh and enters the realm of human sin and death without being corrupted by it, instead cleansing what he touches.
4: A revelation of God: "And Jesus said to him, 'See that you say nothing to anyone, but go, show yourself to the priest and offer the gift that Moses commanded, for a proof to them.'" The instruction to follow the Mosaic purification ritual (Leviticus 14:1-32) shows Jesus fulfilling rather than abolishing the Law (Matthew 5:17). The phrase "for a proof to them" (eis martyrion autois) positions the healed man as living testimony to the messianic presence — the priests will have to render a verdict on a cleansed leper that only divine power could produce.
5: A revelation of God: "When he had entered Capernaum, a centurion came forward to him, appealing to him." The approach of a Roman centurion to Jesus anticipates the Gentile mission announced in 28:19 and the centurion's confession at the cross (Matthew 27:54). That a military officer of the occupying power comes with faith and humility to the Jewish teacher from Nazareth illustrates the "first to the Jew, then to the Gentile" pattern of the Gospel — and its extension. The centurion stands at the threshold of the Gentile inclusion.
6: A revelation of God: "Lord, my servant is lying at home paralyzed, suffering terribly." The centurion intercedes for his slave — a posture of patron-client loyalty that Jesus will identify as surpassing anything he has seen in Israel (Matthew 8:10). The Christological bearing: Christ receives intercession for the helpless; the suffering servant (the centurion's slave) is healed through the faith and intercession of another, mirroring the Isaianic Servant who heals through his own suffering on behalf of others (Isaiah 53:4-5).
7: A revelation of God: "And he said to him, 'I will come and heal him.'" Jesus' readiness to enter a Gentile home (which would have incurred ritual impurity) anticipates the Gentile mission and foreshadows Acts 10 (Peter and Cornelius). The simple declaration "I will come" mirrors YHWH's pattern of coming to save — from the descent to Egypt (Exodus 3:8) to the promise of divine healing in Isa 35. The healer is now present and willing.
8: A direct revelation: "But the centurion replied, 'Lord, I am not worthy to have you come under my roof, but only say the word, and my servant will be healed.'" The centurion's recognition that Jesus' word is sufficient — operating at a distance without physical contact — is a recognition of divine prerogative. YHWH creates by speaking (Genesis 1:3), sustains by his word (Psalms 147:15-18), and heals by his word (Psalms 107:20). The centurion understands more about the nature of Jesus' authority than the crowds who followed the miracles.
9: A revelation of God: "For I too am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. And I say to one, 'Go,' and he goes, and to another, 'Come,' and he comes, and to my servant, 'Do this,' and he does it." The military chain-of-command analogy identifies Jesus as one with ultimate authority — authority that creation, disease, and death obey. The centurion grasps what the disciples will articulate after the stilling of the storm (Matthew 8:27): "what sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him?" Authority over creation is a YHWH prerogative.
10: A direct teaching: "When Jesus heard this, he marveled and said to those who followed him, 'Truly, I say to you, with no one in Israel have I found such faith.'" Christ pronounces on the quality of faith from a Gentile — a judgment he alone is authorized to make. The astonishment of Jesus is the highest form of commendation in the Gospels, occurring here and at the Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:28). The Gentile's faith is superior precisely because it is based on reasoning from Jesus' authority rather than on having witnessed miracles.
11: A direct fulfillment: "I tell you, many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven." The eschatological banquet with the patriarchs directly fulfills the vision of Isaiah 25:6-8 (the mountain feast for all peoples) and Isaiah 49:12 (people coming from east and west and north). The "reclining at table" anticipates the messianic banquet of 26:29 and the wedding feast of Matthew 22:1-14. Christ himself is the host and the one who sends the invitations to all nations.
12: A direct teaching: "While the sons of the kingdom will be thrown into the outer darkness. In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth." The reversal — Gentiles at the patriarchs' table, "sons of the kingdom" excluded — echoes the woes of Isaiah 65:13-15 where those who rejected YHWH are displaced by those who sought him. The "outer darkness" and "weeping and gnashing of teeth" appear throughout Matthew's judgment discourse; the Christological point is that the one who pronounces this verdict is the very one they rejected.
13: A direct fulfillment: "And to the centurion Jesus said, 'Go; let it be done for you as you have believed.' And the servant was healed at that very moment." The healing at a distance at the moment of Jesus' word fulfills Psalms 107:20 ("He sent out his word and healed them, and delivered them from their destruction"). The Christological claim in the structure of the event: YHWH heals by his word; Jesus heals by his word; the identification is made without explicit statement. The centurion's faith is vindicated immediately — faith in the word of Christ is not disappointed.
14: A shadow: "And when Jesus entered Peter's house, he saw his mother-in-law lying sick with a fever." The entry into a private home to heal a bedridden woman echoes Elijah's healing ministry within domestic settings (1 Kings 17:17-24). Jesus' healing ministry in homes — the primary social unit of the ancient world — shows the Incarnation touching every dimension of ordinary life. The healing of Peter's mother-in-law is the first miracle in a private household, and her response (she rose and served him) is the pattern of renewed life in Christ.
15: A shadow: "He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she rose and served him." The laying on of hands for healing (and the immediate restoration to service) echoes Elisha's healing of the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:34-36). The verb "served" (diēkonei) is discipleship vocabulary — the healed woman immediately becomes a servant, the pattern of those who have been touched by Christ's power. Service as the response to healing is the pattern of resurrection life throughout the Gospel (Matthew 20:28: the Son of Man came to serve).
16: A fulfillment: "That evening they brought to him many who were oppressed by demons, and he cast out the spirits with a word and healed all who were sick." The healing of "all" (pantas) who were sick fulfills the scope of Isaiah 53:4 (cited in Matthew 8:17) and anticipates the universal scope of the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19). The casting out of demons "with a word" repeats the authority-through-speech motif: the one who creates and sustains by his word also expels the powers of darkness by his word.
18: A theme: "Now when Jesus saw a crowd around him, he gave orders to go over to the other side." The departure to "the other side" of the Sea of Galilee represents the first movement toward Gentile territory (Decapolis, reached in Matthew 8:28). The Christological shape of this movement: Jesus continually crosses boundaries — geographic, ethnic, purity-related — that others respect. Every crossing anticipates the ultimate boundary-crossing of death and resurrection.
19: A revelation of God: "And a scribe came up and said to him, 'Teacher, I will follow you wherever you go.'" The scribe's offer of unconditional following is met with a warning rather than an invitation — Jesus tests the depth of the commitment before accepting it. This is characteristic of the Christological demand: discipleship means following into the places where Christ goes, including the place of having nowhere to lay his head (Matthew 8:20), the cross, and beyond. The exchange mirrors Abraham's call (Genesis 12:1: "go to the land I will show you").
20: A direct teaching: "And Jesus said to him, 'Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.'" The homelessness of the Son of Man is a direct Christological statement about the incarnate condition: the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3) entered the world without a permanent home in it (John 1:11: "he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him"). The title "Son of Man" here carries the Danielic weight of universal dominion alongside the humiliation of earthly sojourn.
21: A revelation of God: "Another of the disciples said to him, 'Lord, let me first go and bury my father.'" The demand of discipleship as superior to the primary social obligation (burying one's father) shows Christ placing his claim above even the most sacred family duties of the ancient world. This is not callousness but the kingdom's priority claim: the dead burying the dead (Matthew 8:22) contrasts the life of discipleship with the realm of death. Christ's call is a call from death to life (John 5:24-25), which is a higher urgency than the burial of the physically dead.
22: A direct teaching: "And Jesus said to him, 'Follow me, and leave the dead to bury their own dead.'" The sharp demand — "follow me" in priority over "bury your father" — establishes Christ's call as the ultimate obligation, superseding all other obligations however legitimate. The Christological frame: the call to discipleship is the call to cross from death to life, and that crossing cannot be deferred. The formulation "the dead bury their own dead" implies that those outside Christ's life are already in the realm of death.
23: A type: "And when he got into the boat, his disciples followed him." The boat on the sea of Galilee becomes the arena for the revelation of Christ's authority over creation. The OT background: God's mastery over the sea is the mark of the Creator throughout the Psalms (Psalms 65:7; Matthew 89:9; Matthew 107:23-30) and the prophets (Isaiah 51:9-10). The disciples following Jesus into the boat — as he commands the elements — mirrors the entry into the ark (Genesis 7:7) and the wilderness journey: following the one who controls the waters.
24: A type: "And behold, there arose a great storm on the sea, so that the boat was being swamped by the waves; but he was asleep." The sleeping Jesus in the storm echoes Jonah asleep in the hold of the ship during a great storm (Jonah 1:4-5) — but with the key reversal: Jonah was the cause of the storm; Jesus is the one who calms it. The Jonah typology will be made explicit in Matthew 12:39-41 ("the sign of Jonah"). The disciples' panic contrasts with Jesus' rest — he who has authority over the storm can sleep without fear.
25: A type: "And they went and woke him, saying, 'Save us, Lord; we are perishing.'" The cry of the disciples — "Save us, Lord" (sōson, Kyrie) — is the language of the Psalms of lament directed to YHWH: Psalms 107:28-29 ("Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress. He made the storm be still, and the waves of the sea were hushed"). The disciples address Jesus with the title reserved for YHWH's saving acts; they cry out in exactly the right direction without yet grasping the full significance.
26: A direct revelation: "And he said to them, 'Why are you afraid, O you of little faith?' Then he rose and rebuked the winds and the sea, and there was a great calm." The rebuke of wind and sea (epetimēsen tois anemois kai tē thalassē) uses the language of divine command over creation in the OT (Psalms 104:7: "at your rebuke they flee"; Nahum 1:4: "He rebukes the sea and makes it dry"). Only YHWH rebukes the sea in the OT; the disciples' question in Matthew 8:27 ("what sort of man is this?") is the right question, and the Gospel's answer is the Incarnation.
27: A direct revelation: "And the men marveled, saying, 'What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him?'" The disciples' question identifies the event as theophanic — the OT question about extraordinary divine power placed before Jesus' person. In Psalms 107:29-31, those delivered from the storm praise YHWH; here the disciples' praise will eventually be directed to Christ as YHWH incarnate. The question "what sort of man" (potapos) is the Christological question the Gospel has been building toward since the baptism (Matthew 3:17: "This is my beloved Son").
28: A shadow: "And when he came to the other side, to the country of the Gadarenes, two demon-possessed men met him, coming out of the tombs, so that no one could pass that way." The Decapolis region (Gentile territory east of the Sea) and the dwelling-in-tombs detail establish the setting as the realm of maximum uncleanness: Gentile soil, the domain of the dead, the habitation of demonic power. Christ crossing into this territory — unasked — is the shadow of the Incarnation pattern: he enters the realm of darkness, death, and exile to restore it.
29: A direct revelation: "And behold, they cried out, 'What have you to do with us, O Son of God? Have you come here to torment us before the time?'" The demons' recognition of Jesus as "Son of God" and their reference to "the time" of judgment (the eschatological confrontation they know is coming) identifies the cosmic dimension of the exorcisms. The "time" of final judgment and the "time" of Jesus' arrival in Gadara are related: his presence is the in-breaking of the end, a preliminary judgment that anticipates the final one. The demons know the Christological truth that the humans around Jesus are still questioning.
30: A shadow: "Now a herd of many pigs was feeding at some distance from them." The presence of pigs in Gentile territory marks the region as unclean within Jewish categories. The swine will become the vessel into which the demons transfer — a detail that connects to the OT imagery of impure animals as symbols of the nations estranged from God. The Gadarene scene is a miniature of the cosmic situation: the Gentile world as terrain dominated by powers hostile to God, into which the Son of God has come.
31: A shadow: "And the demons begged him, saying, 'If you cast us out, send us away into the herd of pigs.'" The demonic recognition of Christ's authority — they must beg permission rather than resist — shows the absolute sovereignty of the Son of God over the spiritual powers. The Christological bearing: the exorcisms are preliminary victories in the cosmic war that reaches its climax at the cross and resurrection, where "the ruler of this world" is cast out (John 12:31) and the powers are disarmed (Colossians 2:15).
32: A shadow: "And he said to them, 'Go.' So they came out and went into the pigs, and behold, the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned in the waters." The expulsion of the demons and the drowning of the swine in the sea echoes the Exodus typology: Pharaoh's forces drowned in the sea (Exodus 14:27-28) when YHWH delivered his people from demonic bondage. The sea that claims the swine (and the demons) is the same sea that Jesus just stilled — both events demonstrate sovereignty over the chaos of evil.
33: A revelation of God: "The herdsmen fled, and going into the city they told everything, especially what had happened to the demon-possessed men." The herdsmen's report to the city is the first act of proclamation in Gentile territory — a preview of the Gentile mission. The focus "especially what happened to the demon-possessed men" identifies the liberation of the demon-possessed as the central miracle: the restoration of human beings from demonic bondage to their right minds is what the Incarnation accomplishes at every level.
34: A revelation of God: "And behold, all the city came out to meet Jesus, and when they saw him, they begged him to leave their region." The rejection of Jesus by the Gadarenes — preferring the loss of the pigs to the presence of the Son of God — anticipates the pattern of rejection in Israel (Matthew 11:20-24) and the crucifixion. The city comes out to meet him (a quasi-royal reception posture) but asks him to leave — exactly the pattern of the triumphal entry followed by the passion. The Gentiles' rejection here is reversed at the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19): the nations who refused will receive the disciples who come to them.
